\index{Foam@{Foam}!highOrderModeIndices@{highOrderModeIndices}}
\index{highOrderModeIndices@{highOrderModeIndices}!Foam@{Foam}}
\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{highOrderModeIndices()}{highOrderModeIndices()}\hspace{0.1cm}{\footnotesize\ttfamily [1/2]}}
{\footnotesize\ttfamily \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}} $>$ Foam\+::high\+Order\+Mode\+Indices (\begin{DoxyParamCaption}\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}}}]{p\+Order,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{dgGeomCell_2dgCellType_8H_afaeb14dfc0cfab52916039ab825f34d5}{dg\+Cell\+Type}}}]{type,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}}}]{n\+Shells,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}} \&}]{n\+Dof }\end{DoxyParamCaption})\hspace{0.3cm}{\ttfamily [inline]}}



Return modal indices in the highest polynomial shells. 

This helper exposes the basis-\/ordering convention used by the DG modal basis functions. It is intended for sensors and limiters that need to inspect the highest-\/order modal content without duplicating topology-\/specific indexing logic.

For simplex-\/like spaces (tetrahedron and the current hexahedron basis), the modal shell is identified by $i+j+k$. For prisms it is $\max(i,j+k)$, and for pyramids it is $\max(i,j)+k$.


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em p\+Order} & Polynomial order. \\
\hline
{\em type} & DG cell type. \\
\hline
{\em n\+Shells} & Number of highest modal shells to include. A value of 1 selects only the order-\/ $p$ shell. \\
\hline
{\em n\+Dof} & Output total number of modal Do\+Fs for this topology/order. \\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\begin{DoxyReturn}{Returns}
List of selected modal indices in basis-\/function storage order. 
\end{DoxyReturn}
\Hypertarget{namespaceFoam_ae7ae79c79701ad8bec4429d78bcf7a4f}\label{namespaceFoam_ae7ae79c79701ad8bec4429d78bcf7a4f}

{\footnotesize\ttfamily \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{void}} Foam\+::compute\+Hex\+Basis\+And\+Derivatives (\begin{DoxyParamCaption}\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{vector}}}]{eta\+Pt,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}}}]{p\+Order,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{basis,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+dx,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+dy,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+dz }\end{DoxyParamCaption})\hspace{0.3cm}{\ttfamily [inline]}}



Evaluate orthonormal modal basis functions for hexahedral cells. 

This routine evaluates an L2-\/orthonormal modal basis defined on the reference hexahedron using tensor products of Legendre polynomials.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as\+:

\[
  \psi_{i,j,k}(x,y,z)
  =
  \sqrt{\frac{(2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+1)}{8}}
  \;
  P_i(x)\, P_j(y)\, P_k(z),
  \qquad i + j + k \le p
\]

where\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item $P_n$ is the Legendre polynomial of degree $n$,
\item $(x,y,z) \in [-1,1]^3$ is the reference hexahedral domain,
\item the normalization factor ensures L²-\/orthonormality\+:
\end{DoxyItemize}

\[
  \int_{[-1,1]^3}
  \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l}
  \; dx\,dy\,dz
  =
  \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}.
\]

Therefore, the mass matrix on the reference element is exactly the identity matrix when exact integration is used.

The routine evaluates\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a given point,
\item and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
\end{DoxyItemize}


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em eta\+Pt} & Reference coordinates (x,y,z) in \mbox{[}-\/1,1\mbox{]}\texorpdfstring{$^\wedge$}{\string^}3 \\
\hline
{\em p\+Order} & Maximum total polynomial degree \\
\hline
{\em basis} & Values of the orthonormal basis functions \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+dx} & Derivatives with respect to x \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+dy} & Derivatives with respect to y \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+dz} & Derivatives with respect to z \\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\Hypertarget{namespaceFoam_a572c92c5c085d5804bdb33e46a35e840}\label{namespaceFoam_a572c92c5c085d5804bdb33e46a35e840} 
\index{Foam@{Foam}!computePrismBasisAndDerivatives@{computePrismBasisAndDerivatives}}
\index{computePrismBasisAndDerivatives@{computePrismBasisAndDerivatives}!Foam@{Foam}}
\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{computePrismBasisAndDerivatives()}{computePrismBasisAndDerivatives()}}
{\footnotesize\ttfamily \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{void}} Foam\+::compute\+Prism\+Basis\+And\+Derivatives (\begin{DoxyParamCaption}\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{vector}}}]{eta\+Pt,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}}}]{p\+Order,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{basis,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+dx,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+dy,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+dz }\end{DoxyParamCaption})\hspace{0.3cm}{\ttfamily [inline]}}



Evaluate L2-\/orthonormal modal basis functions for prism (wedge) cells. 

This routine evaluates an L²-\/orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference prism element. The construction follows a Duffy-\/type tensor-\/product formulation combining\+:


\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item Legendre polynomials in the η1-\/direction,
\item collapsed Jacobi polynomials in the (η2,η3) triangular structure.
\end{DoxyItemize}

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as\+:

\[
  \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3)
  =
  \sqrt{
    \frac{
      (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2j+2)
    }{8}
  }
  \;
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\;
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\;
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j
  P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3),
\]

with index constraints

\[
  0 \le i \le p, \qquad
  0 \le j \le p, \qquad
  0 \le k \le p-j.
\]

where\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item $P_n^{\alpha,\beta}$ is the Jacobi polynomial of degree n,
\item $(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3$ is the collapsed prism domain,
\item the factor $\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j$ enforces the triangular structure in the (η2,η3) directions,
\item the normalization factor guarantees L²-\/orthonormality.
\end{DoxyItemize}

The basis satisfies\+:

\[
  \int_{\text{ref prism}}
  \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l}
  \; d\eta
  =
  \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}.
\]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-\/element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

\DoxyHorRuler{0}
 \hypertarget{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md1}{}\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Note}{Note}}\label{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md1}
Another commonly used orthogonal wedge basis is written in the form\+:

\[
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\;
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\;
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j
  P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3),
  \qquad i \le p,\; j+k \le p,
\]

without explicit normalization.

This alternative formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference\+: \href{https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/wedge.php}{\texttt{ https\+://www.\+math.\+u-\/bordeaux.\+fr/\texorpdfstring{$\sim$}{\string~}durufle/montjoie/wedge.\+php}}

A detailed comparison between the present L²-\/orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

\DoxyHorRuler{0}


The routine evaluates\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
\item and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
\end{DoxyItemize}


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em eta\+Pt} & Reference coordinates (η1,η2,η3) \\
\hline
{\em p\+Order} & Maximum polynomial order \\
\hline
{\em basis} & Values of the orthonormal basis functions \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+dx} & Derivatives with respect to η1 \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+dy} & Derivatives with respect to η2 \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+dz} & Derivatives with respect to η3\\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
This routine evaluates an L²-\/orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference prism element. The construction follows a Duffy-\/type tensor-\/product formulation combining\+:


\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item Legendre polynomials in the η1-\/direction,
\item collapsed Jacobi polynomials in the (η2,η3) triangular structure.
\end{DoxyItemize}

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as\+:

\[
  \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3)
  =
  \sqrt{
    \frac{
      (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2j+2)
    }{8}
  }
  \;
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\;
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\;
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j
  P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3),
\]

with index constraints

\[
  0 \le i \le p, \qquad
  0 \le j \le p, \qquad
  0 \le k \le p-j.
\]

where\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item $P_n^{\alpha,\beta}$ is the Jacobi polynomial of degree n,
\item $(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3$ is the collapsed prism domain,
\item the factor $\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j$ enforces the triangular structure in the (η2,η3) directions,
\item the normalization factor guarantees L²-\/orthonormality.
\end{DoxyItemize}

The basis satisfies\+:

\[
  \int_{\text{ref prism}}
  \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l}
  \; d\eta
  =
  \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}.
\]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-\/element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

\DoxyHorRuler{0}
 \hypertarget{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md7}{}\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Note}{Note}}\label{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md7}
Another commonly used orthogonal wedge basis is written in the form\+:

\[
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\;
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\;
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^j
  P_k^{2j+1,0}(\eta_3),
  \qquad i \le p,\; j+k \le p,
\]

without explicit normalization.

This alternative formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference\+: \href{https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/wedge.php}{\texttt{ https\+://www.\+math.\+u-\/bordeaux.\+fr/\texorpdfstring{$\sim$}{\string~}durufle/montjoie/wedge.\+php}}

A detailed comparison between the present L²-\/orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{computePyramidBasisAndDerivatives()}{computePyramidBasisAndDerivatives()}}
{\footnotesize\ttfamily \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{void}} Foam\+::compute\+Pyramid\+Basis\+And\+Derivatives (\begin{DoxyParamCaption}\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{vector}}}]{eta\+Pt,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}}}]{p\+Order,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{basis,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+deta1,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+deta2,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+deta3 }\end{DoxyParamCaption})\hspace{0.3cm}{\ttfamily [inline]}}



Evaluate L2-\/orthonormal modal basis functions for pyramid cells. 

This routine evaluates an L²-\/orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference pyramid element. The construction follows the standard collapsed-\/coordinate formulation using Jacobi polynomials to ensure regular behaviour at the apex.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as\+:

\[
  \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3)
  =
  \sqrt{
    \frac{
      (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2(i+j)+3)
    }{8}
  }
  \;
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\,
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\,
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}\,
  P_k^{2(i+j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3),
\]

with index ranges

\[
  0 \le k \le p, \qquad
  0 \le i,j \le p-k.
\]

where\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item $P_n^{\alpha,\beta}$ denotes the Jacobi polynomial,
\item $(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3$ is the collapsed cube domain,
\item the factor $\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}$ enforces the pyramid collapse and guarantees regularity at the apex,
\item the normalization factor guarantees L²-\/orthonormality.
\end{DoxyItemize}

The basis satisfies\+:

\[
  \int_{\text{ref pyramid}}
  \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l}
  \; d\eta
  =
  \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}.
\]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-\/element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

\DoxyHorRuler{0}
 \hypertarget{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md4}{}\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Note}{Note}}\label{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md4}
Another commonly used orthogonal pyramid basis is written as\+:

\[
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\,
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\,
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{\max(i,j)}\,
  P_k^{2\max(i,j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3),
\]

with index constraints

\[
  i \le p,\quad j \le p,\quad k \le p-j.
\]

This formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference\+: \href{https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/pyramid.php}{\texttt{ https\+://www.\+math.\+u-\/bordeaux.\+fr/\texorpdfstring{$\sim$}{\string~}durufle/montjoie/pyramid.\+php}}

A detailed comparison between the present L²-\/orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

\DoxyHorRuler{0}


The routine evaluates\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
\item and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
\end{DoxyItemize}


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em eta\+Pt} & Reference coordinates (η1,η2,η3) \\
\hline
{\em p\+Order} & Maximum polynomial order \\
\hline
{\em basis} & Values of the orthonormal basis functions \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+deta1} & Derivatives with respect to η1 \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+deta2} & Derivatives with respect to η2 \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+deta3} & Derivatives with respect to η3\\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
This routine evaluates an L²-\/orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference pyramid element. The construction follows the standard collapsed-\/coordinate formulation using Jacobi polynomials to ensure regular behaviour at the apex.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as\+:

\[
  \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3)
  =
  \sqrt{
    \frac{
      (2i+1)(2j+1)(2k+2(i+j)+3)
    }{8}
  }
  \;
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\,
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\,
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}\,
  P_k^{2(i+j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3),
\]

with index ranges

\[
  0 \le k \le p, \qquad
  0 \le i,j \le p-k.
\]

where\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item $P_n^{\alpha,\beta}$ denotes the Jacobi polynomial,
\item $(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3$ is the collapsed cube domain,
\item the factor $\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}$ enforces the pyramid collapse and guarantees regularity at the apex,
\item the normalization factor guarantees L²-\/orthonormality.
\end{DoxyItemize}

The basis satisfies\+:

\[
  \int_{\text{ref pyramid}}
  \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l}
  \; d\eta
  =
  \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}.
\]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-\/element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

\DoxyHorRuler{0}
 \hypertarget{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md10}{}\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Note}{Note}}\label{namespaceFoam_autotoc_md10}
Another commonly used orthogonal pyramid basis is written as\+:

\[
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)\,
  P_j^{0,0}(\eta_2)\,
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{\max(i,j)}\,
  P_k^{2\max(i,j)+2,\,0}(\eta_3),
\]

with index constraints

\[
  i \le p,\quad j \le p,\quad k \le p-j.
\]

This formulation appears in the Montjoie finite element framework and related spectral/hp literature.

Reference\+: \href{https://www.math.u-bordeaux.fr/~durufle/montjoie/pyramid.php}{\texttt{ https\+://www.\+math.\+u-\/bordeaux.\+fr/\texorpdfstring{$\sim$}{\string~}durufle/montjoie/pyramid.\+php}}

A detailed comparison between the present L²-\/orthonormal scaling and the Montjoie implementation will be investigated in future work.

\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{computeTetBasisAndDerivatives()}{computeTetBasisAndDerivatives()}}
{\footnotesize\ttfamily \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{void}} Foam\+::compute\+Tet\+Basis\+And\+Derivatives (\begin{DoxyParamCaption}\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{vector}}}]{eta\+Pt,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{label}}}]{p\+Order,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{basis,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+deta1,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+deta2,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{namespaceFoam_a323f15688e458a7ab5bcca56fe1816c8}{scalar}} $>$ \&}]{d\+Basis\+\_\+deta3 }\end{DoxyParamCaption})\hspace{0.3cm}{\ttfamily [inline]}}



Evaluate L2-\/orthonormal modal basis functions for tetrahedral cells. 

This routine evaluates an L²-\/orthonormal hierarchical modal basis defined on the reference tetrahedron. The polynomial space coincides with the classical tetrahedral finite element space of total degree less than or equal to p, but the basis is constructed to be orthonormal with respect to the L² inner product.

The orthonormal basis functions are defined as\+:

\[
  \psi_{i,j,k}(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3)
  =
  \sqrt{
    \frac{
      (2i+1)
      (2j+2i+2)
      (2k+2i+2j+3)
    }{8}
  }
  \;
  P_i^{0,0}(\eta_1)
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_2}{2}\right)^i
  P_j^{2i+1,0}(\eta_2)
  \left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}
  P_k^{2i+2j+2,0}(\eta_3),
  \qquad i + j + k \le p,
\]

where\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item $P_n^{\alpha,\beta}$ denotes the Jacobi polynomial,
\item $(\eta_1,\eta_2,\eta_3) \in [-1,1]^3$ is the collapsed cube domain,
\item the factors $\left(\frac{1-\eta_2}{2}\right)^i$ and $\left(\frac{1-\eta_3}{2}\right)^{i+j}$ induce the tetrahedral collapse structure,
\item the normalization factor guarantees L²-\/orthonormality.
\end{DoxyItemize}

The basis satisfies\+:

\[
  \int_{\text{ref tetra}}
  \psi_{i,j,k}\,\psi_{m,n,l}
  \; d\eta
  =
  \delta_{im}\,\delta_{jn}\,\delta_{kl}.
\]

Therefore, with exact integration, the reference-\/element mass matrix is the identity matrix.

The routine evaluates\+:
\begin{DoxyItemize}
\item the values of all orthonormal modal basis functions at a point,
\item and their first derivatives with respect to each reference coordinate.
\end{DoxyItemize}


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em eta\+Pt} & Reference coordinates (η1,η2,η3) \\
\hline
{\em p\+Order} & Maximum polynomial order \\
\hline
{\em basis} & Values of the orthonormal basis functions \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+deta1} & Derivatives with respect to η1 \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+deta2} & Derivatives with respect to η2 \\
\hline
{\em d\+Basis\+\_\+deta3} & Derivatives with respect to η3 \\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\Hypertarget{namespaceFoam_a1b943de77fdb1c2d70e3d6f447a2bd9d}\label{namespaceFoam_a1b943de77fdb1c2d70e3d6f447a2bd9d} 
\index{Foam@{Foam}!operator\texorpdfstring{$\ast$}{*}@{operator\texorpdfstring{$\ast$}{*}}}

\end{DoxyParams}

Map a point from reference square \mbox{[}-\/1,1\mbox{]}\texorpdfstring{$^\wedge$}{\string^}2 to reference triangle. 

Applies the Duffy transformation to collapse one edge of the square into the triangle apex\+: a = (1 + xi)/2 b = (1 + eta)/2 r = a s = b\texorpdfstring{$\ast$}{*}(1 -\/ a)


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em xi} & First square coordinate \\
\hline
{\em eta} & Second square coordinate\\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\begin{DoxyReturn}{Returns}
Triangle coordinates (r, s) 
\end{DoxyReturn}

Map a physical point to reference coordinates of a trilinear hex. 

Solves x = sum\+\_\+i N\+\_\+i(eta) Xi using Newton iteration. The mapping is trilinear, therefore non-\/affine.


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em x} & Physical point \\
\hline
{\em cell\+Vertices} & List of 8 hex vertices \mbox{[}A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H\mbox{]}\\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\begin{DoxyReturn}{Returns}
Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3) 
\end{DoxyReturn}

Map a physical point to reference coordinates of a 6-\/node prism. 

Solves x = sum\+\_\+i N\+\_\+i(eta) Xi using Newton iteration. The mapping is trilinear and non-\/affine.


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}

\hline
{\em cell\+Vertices} & List of 6 prism vertices \mbox{[}E,A,B,F,D,C\mbox{]}\\

\end{DoxyParams}

Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3) 
\end{DoxyReturn}

Mapping a point from reference space (eta) to physical space (x) for a tetrahedral element. 


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em eta} & Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3) \\
\hline
{\em cell\+Vertices} & Physical coordinates of the tetrahedron vertices (ordered as A, B, C, D) \\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\Hypertarget{namespaceFoam_adb1a87027f715a032834ac94470883c1}\label{namespaceFoam_adb1a87027f715a032834ac94470883c1} 
\index{Foam@{Foam}!mapXToEtaTet@{mapXToEtaTet}}
\index{mapXToEtaTet@{mapXToEtaTet}!Foam@{Foam}}
\doxysubsubsection{\texorpdfstring{mapXToEtaTet()}{mapXToEtaTet()}}
{\footnotesize\ttfamily \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{vector}} Foam\+::map\+XTo\+Eta\+Tet (\begin{DoxyParamCaption}\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{vector}} \&}]{x,  }\item[{\mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{const}} \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{List}}$<$ \mbox{\hyperlink{classFoam_1_1GaussField}{vector}} $>$ \&}]{cell\+Vertices }\end{DoxyParamCaption})\hspace{0.3cm}{\ttfamily [inline]}}



Map a physical point to collapsed reference coordinates of a tetrahedron. 

Computes barycentric coordinates by solving the affine mapping\+:

x = A + λB (B-\/A) + λC (C-\/A) + λD (D-\/A)

Then converts barycentric values to collapsed coordinates\+:

eta3 = 2\texorpdfstring{$\ast$}{*}λD -\/ 1 eta2 = 2\texorpdfstring{$\ast$}{*}λ\+C/(1-\/λD) -\/ 1 eta1 = 2\texorpdfstring{$\ast$}{*}λ\+B/(1-\/λ\+C-\/λD) -\/ 1


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em x} & Physical point \\
\hline
{\em cell\+Vertices} & List of 4 tetra vertices \mbox{[}A,B,C,D\mbox{]}\\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\begin{DoxyReturn}{Returns}
Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3) 
\end{DoxyReturn}
\Hypertarget{namespaceFoam_a90cff712c8f2409d11994753b40760ce}\label{namespaceFoam_a90cff712c8f2409d11994753b40760ce}

Map a physical point to reference coordinates of a 5-\/node pyramid. 

Solves x = sum\+\_\+i N\+\_\+i(eta) Xi using Newton iteration. The mapping is trilinear and non-\/affine.


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em x} & Physical point \\
\hline
{\em cell\+Vertices} & List of 5 pyramid vertices \mbox{[}A,B,C,D,E\mbox{]}\\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\begin{DoxyReturn}{Returns}
Reference coordinates (eta1, eta2, eta3) 
\end{DoxyReturn}

Map reference triangle coordinates to physical space. 

Computes the physical point X from barycentric coordinates (r, s) on the reference triangle using\+:

X = (1 -\/ r -\/ s) P0 + r P1 + s P2


\begin{DoxyParams}{Parameters}
{\em rs} & Reference coordinates (r, s) in the reference triangle \\
\hline
{\em vertices} & Physical coordinates of the triangle vertices (ordered as A, B, C)\\
\hline
\end{DoxyParams}
\begin{DoxyReturn}{Returns}
Physical 3D coordinate 
\end{DoxyReturn}
